# Google bakes NotebookLM, its research tool, into Gemini

## You can now create NotebookLM notebooks within the chatbot.

[Mariella Moon](https://www.engadget.com/about/editors/mariella-moon/)

Contributing Reporter

Thu, April 9, 2026 at 10:18 AM UTC

[Add Engadget on Google](https://www.google.com/preferences/source?q=engadget.com)

Thu, April 9, 2026 at 10:18 AM UTC

![Image 2](https://s.yimg.com/ny/api/res/1.2/SuieT8rkPIbrmpT1twFfWQ--/YXBwaWQ9aGlnaGxhbmRlcjt3PTEyNDI7aD03MDA-/https://s.yimg.com/uu/api/res/1.2/uiQbnEe03MlUZ9tvyEgWtQ--~B/aD05MDI7dz0xNjAwO2FwcGlkPXl0YWNoeW9u/https://d29szjachogqwa.cloudfront.net/images/user-uploaded/google-gemini-notebooks_3238.jpg)

Google (Google)

Google has [fully integrated NotebookLM](https://blog.google/innovation-and-ai/products/gemini-app/notebooks-gemini-notebooklm/), its AI-powered research tool, into the Gemini app. The company launched a [standalone NotebookLM app](https://www.engadget.com/apps/notebooklm-the-acceptable-face-of-google-ai-is-getting-an-app-in-may-182041294.html) last year, but as it said in its announcement, “keeping track of everything can be a challenge.” It added NotebookLM as a source in the Gemini app last year, but now you can create notebooks right inside the chatbot.

https://www.simplenews.ai/news/anthropic.title: Anthropic Blocks Third-Party Harnesses Like OpenClaw from Claude Subscriptions
https://www.simplenews.ai/news/anthropic.text:
URL Source: https://www.simplenews.ai/news/anthropic-blocks-third-party-harnesses-like-openclaw-from-claude-subscriptions-261x

Published Time: 2026-04-04T01:13:14.373+00:00

Markdown Content:
Anthropic announced on April 3, 2026 that subscribers can no longer use their Claude subscription limits with third-party harnesses like [OpenClaw](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw), effective April 4 at 12pm PT. The policy requires users to enable pay-as-you-go "extra usage" billed separately from subscriptions to continue accessing these tools with their Claude accounts.

## Policy Change Affects All Third-Party Harnesses

The restriction applies to all third-party harnesses, not just OpenClaw, though [OpenClaw is the first to face enforcement](https://www.theregister.com/2026/02/20/anthropic_clarifies_ban_third_party_claude_access/). Anthropic's email to subscribers stated: "We've been working to manage demand across the board, but these tools put an outsized strain on our systems. Capacity is a resource we manage carefully and we need to prioritize our customers using our core products."

Subscriptions will continue covering Claude's first-party products including Claude Code and Claude Cowork. Third-party integrations now require separate billing through the extra usage system.

## Financial Assistance and Refund Options Offered

Anthropic is providing several financial accommodations to affected users:

*   One-time credit equal to monthly subscription price (must be redeemed by April 17)
*   Discounts up to 30% on pre-purchased extra usage bundles
*   Full subscription refund option for users preferring to cancel

The announcement comes days after the [Claude Code source leak](https://venturebeat.com/technology/claude-codes-source-code-appears-to-have-leaked-heres-what-we-know) triggered a wave of third-party implementations, raising questions about whether the policy targets this new ecosystem of tools.

## Community Response on Hacker News

The policy generated significant discussion on Hacker News, with the announcement post receiving 187 points and 175 comments. Users are debating the implications for existing workflows, the financial impact of separate billing, and whether this signals broader restrictions on API access and third-party tooling in the AI ecosystem.

The enforcement timeline begins April 4 with OpenClaw, with additional third-party harnesses to be added in subsequent rollouts.

## Key Takeaways

*   Anthropic is blocking third-party harnesses like OpenClaw from using Claude subscription limits starting April 4, 2026
*   Users must enable separate pay-as-you-go "extra usage" billing to continue using these tools
*   The policy applies to all third-party harnesses, with OpenClaw being the first enforced
*   Anthropic cites capacity management and prioritization of core products as the rationale
*   Financial assistance includes one-time credits, up to 30% discounts on usage bundles, and refund options

https://towardsdatascience.com/why-every.title: Why Every AI Coding Assistant Needs a Memory Layer
https://towardsdatascience.com/why-every.text:
URL Source: https://towardsdatascience.com/why-every-ai-coding-assistant-needs-a-memory-layer/

Published Time: 2026-04-11T13:00:00+00:00

Markdown Content:
you start a new chat session with your AI coding assistant (whether that’s Cursor, Claude Code, Windsurf, or Cortex Code), you’re essentially starting from zero.

The AI coding assistant doesn’t know that your team uses [Streamlit](https://streamlit.io/) for building web apps. It also doesn’t know that you prefer Material icons over emojis. And it also doesn’t know about that port conflict that made you switch from `8501` to `8505` three months ago.

So you repeat yourself. Session after session.

The tools are powerful, but they are also forgetful. And until you address this memory gap, you’re the human-in-the-loop who is manually managing state that could otherwise be automated.

## The Stateless Reality of Large language models (LLMs)

LLMs don’t remember you. Each conversation is a blank slate, by architecture and not by accident.

Your conversation lives in a _context window_ with a hard token limit. Once you close the chat, all traces of the conversation is gone. That’s by design for privacy reasons, but it’s a friction for anyone who needs continuity.

Let’s now take a look at the technical differences between short-term and long-term memory:

*   **Short-term memory**: What the AI remembers within a single session. This lives in the context window and includes your current conversation, any open files, and recent actions. When you close the chat, it’s all gone.
*   **Long-term memory**: What persists across sessions. This is what rules files, memory services, and external integrations provide. It’s knowledge that survives beyond a single conversation.

Without long-term memory, you become the memory layer, copy-paste context, assemble the context, re-explain conventions, answer the same clarifying questions that you answered yesterday and the day before that.

This obviously doesn’t scale.

### The Compounding Cost of Repetition

Let’s consider the compounding cost of a lack of persistent memory. But before doing so, we’re going to take a look at what this looks like in practice:

**Without persistent context:**

**You:** Build me a dashboard for this dataAI: Here’s a React dashboard with Chart.js…

**You:**No, I use StreamlitAI: Here’s a Streamlit app with Plotly…

**You:** I prefer Altair for chartsAI: Here’s the Altair version…

**You:** Can you use wide layout?AI: [finally produces something usable after 4 corrections]

**With persistent context (rules file):**

**You:**Build me a dashboard for this data

**AI:** [reads your rules file, knows your tech stack and preferences]

Here’s a Streamlit dashboard with wide layout and Altair charts…

As you can see from both examples, same requests but dramatically different experiences. The AI with context produces usable code on the first try because it already knows your preferences.
